---
title: "Feature Objects"
subtitle: "Three data shapes for measurements, events, and tracked objects over time."
sidebar: concepts
---

## Why there are three object types

Extracted features do not all have the same shape. Loudness might be measured
every 10 milliseconds. A transcript contains words with a beginning and an
end. A face tracker may follow several faces across many video frames. Forcing
all three into the same table would discard useful structure or fill the table
with repeated and missing values.

`natural_features` therefore uses three temporal object types:

| Object | Use it for | A row means |
|---|---|---|
| `FeatureSeries` | Repeated measurements such as loudness, embeddings, or frame descriptors | One measurement time or window |
| `EventSeries` | Bounded events such as words, phonemes, cuts, or detected objects | One event with an onset and an offset |
| `TrackSeries` | The same named objects followed across time | One time step across one or more tracks |

“Temporal” simply means that every row has a stated position in time.

## `FeatureSeries`: a matrix whose first axis is time

A `FeatureSeries` keeps a numeric `values` array next to `times_s`. The first
axis of `values` must have one entry for every time. For example, an array with
shape `(100, 13)` can represent 13 acoustic measurements at each of 100 time
steps. The `dims` field names those axes and must begin with `time`.

A time can represent an instant or a window. If a measurement summarizes the
audio from 1.00 to 1.03 seconds, the object preserves those bounds; it does not
pretend the row is an infinitesimal point.

## `EventSeries`: one onset and offset per event

An `EventSeries` stores matching `onset_s` and `offset_s` arrays. Labels and
confidence values are optional, but when present they must have one value per
event. An event may have zero duration, but its end can never precede its
start.

For a spoken word, one row might mean: label “room,” onset 4.20 seconds,
offset 4.61 seconds, confidence 0.94. The interval is part of the object rather
than an informal convention attached later.

## `TrackSeries`: time by tracked identity

A `TrackSeries` has time on its first axis and tracked identity on its second.
If a video contains 60 frames and three tracked faces, the first two dimensions
of `values` are 60 by 3. `track_id` says which face occupies each position on
the second axis. Additional axes can hold coordinates, appearance features, or
other measurements.

## The information every object carries

Each object names its clock and describes whether its rows are points, fixed
windows, or individually bounded intervals. Each also carries an
`extractor_id`, which names the method that produced it, and a `params_hash`,
which is a short fingerprint of that method's settings. Together these fields
are provenance: information about where an array came from and how it was
made.

The objects are immutable: alignment and clock conversion create a new object
instead of rewriting the original values in place.

## Errors are raised before the object can become ambiguous

Times must be finite and sorted from earliest to latest. Array lengths and axes
must agree. Interval ends must not precede their starts. Required provenance
fields must be present. These checks prevent a malformed temporal object from
silently entering table export or alignment.

## What these objects do not decide

Feature objects preserve extracted data on its original time grid. They do not
choose a database, interpolate to a new rate, convolve a hemodynamic response
function (HRF), or build an fMRI design matrix. `Timeline` and
`FeatureAlignment` describe a relationship to another timeline without
changing the original object.

## See also

[Unified workflow](unified-workflow.qmd) · [Native time and clocks](native-time-and-clocks.qmd) · [Feature object schemas](../spec/feature-object-schemas.qmd) · [API reference](../reference/index.qmd)
